title: The biggest winners of the American economy fear they’re sinking fast
byline: Elizabeth Dwoskin, Shira Ovide, The Washington Post
excerpt: Waves of layoffs and growing pressure to automate jobs are creating existential panic in the tech industry. Economists say other white-collar workers are next.
content_html:
SAN FRANCISCO - Throughout a nearly two-decade career, Susan Smith considered herself a winner in the American economic lottery. She moved easily between tech jobs, earning a salary of more than $500,000 in her most recent position, as a mid-tier executive at Meta. A single mom, Smith could afford two nannies and support her disabled son without financial worry.

But as Silicon Valley has pushed headlong into artificial intelligence and heavy job cuts, that security has evaporated. Smith once readily put in 70-hour workweeks, believing the tech giant’s apps benefited humanity and that her contributions were highly valued. That feeling eroded in her last role, helping executives train new “AI employees,” part of an internal mandate that managers treat AI systems as real team members.

She survived four rounds of layoffs at Meta, but in May, the company let her go.

Now Smith is confronting a job market that looks nothing like that era of plenty. Few positions are available in her field, and several places where she’s applied haven’t called her back. Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in 2020, eventually gave up looking for jobs in his profession. He now lifts boxes at a warehouse.

Since the Great Recession nearly 20 years ago, political and corporate leaders have hailed Silicon Valley as a bright spot of American dynamism as the industry sped away economically from the rest of the United States. Young Americans, particularly those at elite universities, flocked to tech over Wall Street and made computer science by far the fastest-growing large major at U.S. colleges since 2008.

Breaking into the ranks of Meta, Google or SpaceX offered a trapping of cushy excess and entrance to the Silicon Valley corporate religion that a random software widget could change the world.

By most objective measures, America’s tech capitals remain superstar winners of the economy. Only the workers say they don’t feel that way. Their ingrained optimism has given way to a feeling that a Hunger Games-level scarcity is now the new normal.

Smith counts herself among the ranks of the disillusioned, though she said she’s not pessimistic about the technology itself. “I just don’t personally see the point of doing it just to make a couple of people quadruple trillionaires,” she said.

The sense of precariousness among America’s professional elite could be a preview of what’s ahead for the rest of the country as AI spreads. Silicon Valley techies are “are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone,” said Anil Dash, a technology entrepreneur.

At tech companies, leaders obsessed with winning the AI race have tasked their workforces of coders, lawyers and HR professionals with becoming the front line of that transformation. They’re being measured by how quickly they can automate their own jobs while watching their colleagues get pushed out in successive waves of layoffs. Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon. (Amazon Executive Chairman Jeff Bezos owns The Washington Post.)

“There’s this whole tranche of people who’ve been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, ‘Now I’m the guy on the street‚’” said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force.

In the early days of ChatGPT, Raskin’s surveys showed broad optimism, he said. Now he says he believes the sentiment has changed.

“All these people … with some of the most coveted and secure jobs in the upper-middle class … - these are the people who have done the thing that everyone has told them to do: Study hard, get these jobs and then you’ll be rewarded,” Raskin said. “And all of a sudden, they find themselves on the front lines of this transition.”

This existential panic is occurring during what should be a euphoric moment, as tech stocks continue their 15-year growth tear and a set of AI companies, including OpenAI and Anthropic, prepare to go public with stratospheric valuations. (The Post has a content partnership with OpenAI, the creator of ChatGPT.)

But unlike in previous waves of initial public offerings, the financial payoffs of the new IPOs will lift a smaller group of people to staggering wealth, industry veterans say. That’s leaving their already affluent peers worried about their own futures, exacerbating feelings of inequality and insecurity.

[AI is already reshaping U.S. politics at every level]

The professional unease of Silicon Valley’s elite gives them something in common with American workers who have endured decades of eroding workplace security, according to labor market historians.

Autoworkers in the mid-to-late 20th century were that era’s technology workers, said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin. Manufacturing was considered core to the country’s identity and economic vitality, and auto jobs were considered stable, well paying and high status in many communities, he said.

It took about two generations beginning in the 1970s for autoworkers to really lose ground as assembly lines automated, said McCartin, who predicted that AI would hit white-collar work faster than that. “The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically,” McCartin said. “But the way it’s going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry.”

Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy. “Low-wage workers are used to it,” she said.

Meanwhile, AI has transformed tech work into a cutthroat environment, where once-lauded skills are devalued. Companies are now factoring AI use into performance reviews; giants such as Meta, Amazon and the computer chip upstart Cerebras have used visual displays to rank workers by how much AI they use compared to their peers. This practice of using AI as much as possible is now being dialed back as the costly tech blows through company budgets.

People skilled at complex software programming have status and pride in their hard-earned craft, Dash said. But increasingly, coders - some of the most highly paid workers in the tech industry - express fear that automated coding is making some of their expertise irrelevant.

“They have a self-awareness that [their coding skills] will never matter in the world again,” Dash said.

Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say. And they don’t account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment.

Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people.

The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for “novel situations,” “ethical considerations” and “high-stakes moments.”

Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector’s economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year - more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy.

He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people - one that is spreading to other professional classes.

“That’s the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down,” Levanon said.

Anneke Buffone, a psychology Ph.D. who had been recruited to Meta to work on well-being and safety issues before leaving in December, said that programmers aren’t the only ones feeling squeezed.

“There’s just a lot of uncertainty, a lot of fear, a lot of people feeling like, if they get let go from this job, they might not find another one,” she said.

She described getting pushback from managers when she tried to explain that a particular AI tool was a waste of time. The system had so many errors that she needed to redo its work.

But her bosses “were like, ‘No, keep using it more. It’ll just get better,’” she said. “There’s a feeling among management that when you voice that AI has all these problems, they don’t want to hear it.” AI, she said, makes “everything go so much faster. It creates this illusion that you can get all this stuff done. Much of the time you’re just babysitting the AIs.”

A 31-year-old tech start-up worker living in San Francisco, who spoke on the condition of anonymity for fear of professional repercussions, said that her engineering manager husband told her a few months ago that he needed to focus all his energy on becoming an “AI native” and requested that she take on almost all parenting responsibilities for the couple’s preschool-age daughter. She complied.

She described the experience as surreal, as he spent days, nights and weekends, locked in his office toiling away on AI projects. But her husband eventually thanked her: He was now the top user of AI in his company.

Still, the couple, who earn a combined income of $550,000, say they feel woefully behind their friends, many of whom work at OpenAI and Anthropic and are expecting big payouts when their companies go public. “I have this dream house that I see on Redfin but I know I won’t get to it because Anthropic people will get to it first,” she said.

A longtime venture capitalist, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to speak candidly about her financial circumstances, said she and her husband were priced out when looking for a home in San Francisco in the $3 million price range. Many of the homes in her price range bid up to $7 million and $8 million, leading locals here to refer to a “mansion shortage.”

What’s happening in Silicon Valley is a turbocharged version of the division in the American economy between superstars and everyone else.

Superstar companies such as Google, Amazon and Meta won a concentrated share of their markets - and tech stocks have accounted for nearly half of the surge in the S&P 500 since the end of 2010, according to S&P Global Indices. Superstar cities, including in the Bay Area, generate far more economic output per worker and thrive relative to low-productivity ones. About one third of the total personal wealth in the United States is held by the top 1 percent of households, while the bottom half of Americans holds less than 3 percent, according to Federal Reserve data.

Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late ’90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he’s hopeful that it’s an early chapter in an evolving story. “It’s happened many times before,” he said, “that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is - into a new wave of prosperity.”

While still job hunting, Smith is now volunteering at her church, where she has helped church leaders revamp their use of tech with AI.

She’s not sure if she wants to go back to Silicon Valley - or if that’s even possible. “The emperor has no clothes,” she said.

sentences.en: A single mom, Smith could afford two nannies and support her disabled son without financial worry.
sentences.zh: 作为一名单亲妈妈，史密斯可以负担得起两名保姆的费用并抚养她残疾的儿子，而无需担心经济问题。

blocks.html_en: She survived four rounds of layoffs at Meta, but in May, the company let her go.

sentences.en: He now lifts boxes at a warehouse.
sentences.zh: 他现在在仓库搬运箱子。

sentences.en: “I just don’t personally see the point of doing it just to make a couple of people quadruple trillionaires,” she said.

sentences.en: Silicon Valley techies are “are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone,” said Anil Dash, a technology entrepreneur.
sentences.zh: 科技企业家阿尼尔·达什 (Anil Dash) 表示，硅谷技术人员“是技术人员想要为每个人做的事情的小白鼠”。

blocks.html_en: At tech companies, leaders obsessed with winning the AI race have tasked their workforces of coders, lawyers and HR professionals with becoming the front line of that transformation. They’re being measured by how quickly they can automate their own jobs while watching their colleagues get pushed out in successive waves of layoffs. Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon. (Amazon Executive Chairman Jeff Bezos owns The Washington Post.)

sentences.en: At tech companies, leaders obsessed with winning the AI race have tasked their workforces of coders, lawyers and HR professionals with becoming the front line of that transformation.
sentences.zh: 在科技公司，痴迷于赢得人工智能竞赛的领导者已责成他们的程序员、律师和人力资源专业人员成为这场变革的前线。

sentences.en: They’re being measured by how quickly they can automate their own jobs while watching their colleagues get pushed out in successive waves of layoffs.
sentences.zh: 他们的衡量标准是他们能够以多快的速度实现自己的工作自动化，同时看着他们的同事在连续的裁员浪潮中被淘汰。

sentences.en: Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon.
sentences.zh: 追踪已宣布裁员的 Layoffs.fyi 统计，自 2022 年以来，已有超过 80 万名科技员工被解雇，其中包括 Meta、微软、甲骨文和亚马逊近几个月的大规模裁员。

sentences.en: (Amazon Executive Chairman Jeff Bezos owns The Washington Post.)
sentences.zh: （亚马逊执行董事长杰夫·贝佐斯拥有《华盛顿邮报》。）

blocks.html_en: “There’s this whole tranche of people who’ve been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, ‘Now I’m the guy on the street‚’” said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force.

sentences.en: “There’s this whole tranche of people who’ve been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, ‘Now I’m the guy on the street‚’” said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force.
sentences.zh: 奥利弗·拉斯金 (Oliver Raskin) 创立了硅谷市场研究咨询公司 Signalcraft Insights，并对科技劳动力的态度进行了调查。

blocks.html_en: In the early days of ChatGPT, Raskin’s surveys showed broad optimism, he said. Now he says he believes the sentiment has changed.

sentences.en: In the early days of ChatGPT, Raskin’s surveys showed broad optimism, he said.
sentences.zh: 他说，在 ChatGPT 的早期，拉斯金的调查显示出广泛的乐观情绪。

sentences.en: Now he says he believes the sentiment has changed.
sentences.zh: 现在他说他相信这种情绪已经改变。

blocks.html_en: “All these people … with some of the most coveted and secure jobs in the upper-middle class … - these are the people who have done the thing that everyone has told them to do: Study hard, get these jobs and then you’ll be rewarded,” Raskin said. “And all of a sudden, they find themselves on the front lines of this transition.”

sentences.en: “All these people …
sentences.zh: “所有这些人……

sentences.en: with some of the most coveted and secure jobs in the upper-middle class … - these are the people who have done the thing that everyone has told them to do: Study hard, get these jobs and then you’ll be rewarded,” Raskin said.
sentences.zh: 拥有一些中上阶层最令人垂涎​​、最有保障的工作……这些人做了每个人都告诉他们要做的事情：努力学习，得到这些工作，然后你就会得到回报，”拉斯金说。

sentences.en: “And all of a sudden, they find themselves on the front lines of this transition.”
sentences.zh: “突然之间，他们发现自己处于这一转变的最前线。”

blocks.html_en: This existential panic is occurring during what should be a euphoric moment, as tech stocks continue their 15-year growth tear and a set of AI companies, including OpenAI and Anthropic, prepare to go public with stratospheric valuations. (The Post has a content partnership with OpenAI, the creator of ChatGPT.)

sentences.en: This existential panic is occurring during what should be a euphoric moment, as tech stocks continue their 15-year growth tear and a set of AI companies, including OpenAI and Anthropic, prepare to go public with stratospheric valuations.
sentences.zh: 这种存在主义恐慌发生在本应令人欣喜的时刻，科技股继续保持 15 年的增长势头，包括 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 在内的一系列人工智能公司正准备以极高的估值上市。

sentences.en: (The Post has a content partnership with OpenAI, the creator of ChatGPT.)
sentences.zh: （《华盛顿邮报》与 ChatGPT 的创建者 OpenAI 建立了内容合作伙伴关系。）

blocks.html_en: But unlike in previous waves of initial public offerings, the financial payoffs of the new IPOs will lift a smaller group of people to staggering wealth, industry veterans say. That’s leaving their already affluent peers worried about their own futures, exacerbating feelings of inequality and insecurity.

sentences.en: But unlike in previous waves of initial public offerings, the financial payoffs of the new IPOs will lift a smaller group of people to staggering wealth, industry veterans say.
sentences.zh: 但业内资深人士表示，与前几波首次公开募股不同的是，新一轮首次公开募股的财务回报将使一小部分人获得惊人的财富。

sentences.en: That’s leaving their already affluent peers worried about their own futures, exacerbating feelings of inequality and insecurity.
sentences.zh: 这让本已富裕的同龄人对自己的未来感到担忧，加剧了不平等和不安全感。

blocks.html_en: [AI is already reshaping U.S. politics at every level]

sentences.en: [AI is already reshaping U.S. politics at every level]
sentences.zh: [人工智能已经在各个层面重塑美国政治]

blocks.html_en: The professional unease of Silicon Valley’s elite gives them something in common with American workers who have endured decades of eroding workplace security, according to labor market historians.

sentences.en: The professional unease of Silicon Valley’s elite gives them something in common with American workers who have endured decades of eroding workplace security, according to labor market historians.
sentences.zh: 劳动力市场历史学家表示，硅谷精英的职业不安使他们与美国工人有一些共同之处，美国工人几十年来一直在忍受工作场所安全的侵蚀。

blocks.html_en: Autoworkers in the mid-to-late 20th century were that era’s technology workers, said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin. Manufacturing was considered core to the country’s identity and economic vitality, and auto jobs were considered stable, well paying and high status in many communities, he said.

sentences.en: Autoworkers in the mid-to-late 20th century were that era’s technology workers, said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin.
sentences.zh: 乔治城大学历史学家约瑟夫·麦卡汀 (Joseph McCartin) 表示，20 世纪中后期的汽车工人是那个时代的技术工人。

sentences.en: Manufacturing was considered core to the country’s identity and economic vitality, and auto jobs were considered stable, well paying and high status in many communities, he said.
sentences.zh: 他说，制造业被认为是国家身份和经济活力的核心，而汽车工作在许多社区被认为是稳定、高薪和高地位的。

blocks.html_en: It took about two generations beginning in the 1970s for autoworkers to really lose ground as assembly lines automated, said McCartin, who predicted that AI would hit white-collar work faster than that. “The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically,” McCartin said. “But the way it’s going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry.”

sentences.en: It took about two generations beginning in the 1970s for autoworkers to really lose ground as assembly lines automated, said McCartin, who predicted that AI would hit white-collar work faster than that.
sentences.zh: 麦卡汀表示，从 20 世纪 70 年代开始，随着装配线自动化，汽车工人花了大约两代人的时间才真正失去了优势，他预测人工智能将比这更快地影响白领工作。

sentences.en: “The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically,” McCartin said.
sentences.zh: “尤其是人工智能的兴起，必将彻底改变工作场所，”麦卡汀说。

sentences.en: “But the way it’s going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry.”
sentences.zh: “但它发生的方式类似于技术如何改变汽车行业。”

blocks.html_en: Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy. “Low-wage workers are used to it,” she said.

sentences.en: Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy.
sentences.zh: 纽约城市大学的劳工社会学家露丝·米尔克曼 (Ruth Milkman) 表示，技术工人正面临着其他行业工人长期以来抱怨的情况：工作不稳定或剥夺了他们的自主权。

sentences.en: “Low-wage workers are used to it,” she said.
sentences.zh: “低薪工人已经习惯了，”她说。

sentences.en: Code with Claude marquee sign at Anthropic's Code with Claude developer conference on Wednesday, May 6, 2026 in San Francisco.
sentences.zh: 2026 年 5 月 6 日星期三，旧金山，Code with Claude 在 Anthropic 的 Code with Claude 开发者大会上签名。

sentences.en: (Don Feria/AP Content Services for Anthropic)
sentences.zh: （Don Feria/美联社 Anthropic 内容服务）

blocks.html_en: Meanwhile, AI has transformed tech work into a cutthroat environment, where once-lauded skills are devalued. Companies are now factoring AI use into performance reviews; giants such as Meta, Amazon and the computer chip upstart Cerebras have used visual displays to rank workers by how much AI they use compared to their peers. This practice of using AI as much as possible is now being dialed back as the costly tech blows through company budgets.

sentences.en: Meanwhile, AI has transformed tech work into a cutthroat environment, where once-lauded skills are devalued.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，人工智能已将技术工作转变为残酷的环境，曾经备受赞誉的技能被贬低。

sentences.en: Companies are now factoring AI use into performance reviews; giants such as Meta, Amazon and the computer chip upstart Cerebras have used visual displays to rank workers by how much AI they use compared to their peers.
sentences.zh: 公司现在正在将人工智能的使用纳入绩效评估中； Meta、亚马逊和计算机芯片新贵 Cerebras 等巨头已经使用视觉显示器，根据员工与同事相比使用人工智能的程度对他们进行排名。

sentences.en: This practice of using AI as much as possible is now being dialed back as the costly tech blows through company budgets.
sentences.zh: 由于昂贵的技术耗尽了公司的预算，这种尽可能多地使用人工智能的做法现在正在被取消。

blocks.html_en: People skilled at complex software programming have status and pride in their hard-earned craft, Dash said. But increasingly, coders - some of the most highly paid workers in the tech industry - express fear that automated coding is making some of their expertise irrelevant.

sentences.en: People skilled at complex software programming have status and pride in their hard-earned craft, Dash said.
sentences.zh: 达什说，擅长复杂软件编程的人对他们来之不易的技艺拥有地位和自豪。

sentences.en: But increasingly, coders - some of the most highly paid workers in the tech industry - express fear that automated coding is making some of their expertise irrelevant.
sentences.zh: 但越来越多的程序员——科技行业中收入最高的工人——表示担心自动化编码会让他们的一些专业知识变得无关紧要。

blocks.html_en: “They have a self-awareness that [their coding skills] will never matter in the world again,” Dash said.

sentences.en: “They have a self-awareness that [their coding skills] will never matter in the world again,” Dash said.
sentences.zh: “他们有一种自我意识，[他们的编码技能]在世界上将不再重要，”达什说。

blocks.html_en: Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say. And they don’t account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment.

sentences.en: Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say.
sentences.zh: 专家表示，科技公司的许多裁员可能是前几年过度招聘的后遗症。

sentences.en: And they don’t account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment.
sentences.zh: 而且它们并没有考虑到信息行业近期招聘人数的不稳定增长，其中包括软件开发人员的就业以及媒体和娱乐行业的就业机会。

blocks.html_en: Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people.

sentences.en: Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people.
sentences.zh: 不过，深入挖掘后，一些经济学家表示，有迹象表明，硅谷和美国经济中其他依赖技术的部分已经达到了一个转折点，它们在不需要那么多人的情况下实现了增长。

blocks.html_en: The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for “novel situations,” “ethical considerations” and “high-stakes moments.”

sentences.en: The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for “novel situations,” “ethical considerations” and “high-stakes moments.”
sentences.zh: 这个概念在最近一次在整个科技行业群聊中引起反响的演讲中得到了概括：在演讲中，初创企业孵化器 Y Combinator 的一位合作伙伴预示着新一代人工智能优先的公司将只在“新情况”、“道德考虑”和“高风险时刻”需要人力。

blocks.html_en: Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector’s economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year - more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy.

sentences.en: Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector’s economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year - more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy.
sentences.zh: 非营利性劳工研究组织 Burning Glass Institute 首席经济学家加德·莱瓦农 (Gad Levanon) 表示，自 2022 年以来，信息行业的工作时间有所下降，而该行业的经济产出每年增长约 8%，是美国经济总体增长率的三倍多。

blocks.html_en: He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people - one that is spreading to other professional classes.

sentences.en: He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people - one that is spreading to other professional classes.
sentences.zh: 他表示，这些数据揭示了包括技术和金融在内的行业发生的巨大变化，即用相同或更少的人完成更多的工作，这种变化正在蔓延到其他专业阶层。

blocks.html_en: “That’s the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down,” Levanon said.

sentences.en: “That’s the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down,” Levanon said.
sentences.zh: “这就是白领和科技工作的新现实：产出增加，员工人数持平或减少，”莱瓦农说。

sentences.en: Anneke Buffone, who had worked on well-being and safety issues for Meta, left the company in December.
sentences.zh: Anneke Buffone 曾在 Meta 负责福利和安全问题，于 12 月离开公司。

sentences.en: (Bryan Anselm/For The Washington Post)
sentences.zh: （布莱恩·安瑟姆/华盛顿邮报）

blocks.html_en: Anneke Buffone, a psychology Ph.D. who had been recruited to Meta to work on well-being and safety issues before leaving in December, said that programmers aren’t the only ones feeling squeezed.

sentences.en: Anneke Buffone, a psychology Ph.D.
sentences.zh: Anneke Buffone，心理学博士

sentences.en: who had been recruited to Meta to work on well-being and safety issues before leaving in December, said that programmers aren’t the only ones feeling squeezed.
sentences.zh: 去年 12 月离开之前，他被招募到 Meta 负责福利和安全问题的工作，他说程序员并不是唯一感到受到挤压的人。

blocks.html_en: “There’s just a lot of uncertainty, a lot of fear, a lot of people feeling like, if they get let go from this job, they might not find another one,” she said.

sentences.en: “There’s just a lot of uncertainty, a lot of fear, a lot of people feeling like, if they get let go from this job, they might not find another one,” she said.
sentences.zh: “有很多不确定性，很多恐惧，很多人感觉，如果他们被解雇了，他们可能就找不到另一份工作了，”她说。

blocks.html_en: She described getting pushback from managers when she tried to explain that a particular AI tool was a waste of time. The system had so many errors that she needed to redo its work.

sentences.en: She described getting pushback from managers when she tried to explain that a particular AI tool was a waste of time.
sentences.zh: 她描述了当她试图解释某种特定的人工智能工具是浪费时间时，遭到了经理的抵制。

sentences.en: The system had so many errors that she needed to redo its work.
sentences.zh: 系统错误太多，她需要重做。

blocks.html_en: But her bosses “were like, ‘No, keep using it more. It’ll just get better,’” she said. “There’s a feeling among management that when you voice that AI has all these problems, they don’t want to hear it.” AI, she said, makes “everything go so much faster. It creates this illusion that you can get all this stuff done. Much of the time you’re just babysitting the AIs.”

sentences.en: But her bosses “were like, ‘No, keep using it more.
sentences.zh: 但她的老板“说，‘不，继续更多地使用它。

sentences.en: It’ll just get better,’” she said.
sentences.zh: 一切都会变得更好，”她说。

sentences.en: “There’s a feeling among management that when you voice that AI has all these problems, they don’t want to hear it.”
sentences.zh: “管理层有一种感觉，当你说人工智能存在所有这些问题时，他们不想听到。”

sentences.en: AI, she said, makes “everything go so much faster.
sentences.zh: 她说，人工智能让“一切都变得更快。

sentences.en: It creates this illusion that you can get all this stuff done.
sentences.zh: 它创造了一种你可以完成所有这些事情的错觉。

sentences.en: Much of the time you’re just babysitting the AIs.”
sentences.zh: 很多时候你只是在照顾人工智能。”

blocks.html_en: A 31-year-old tech start-up worker living in San Francisco, who spoke on the condition of anonymity for fear of professional repercussions, said that her engineering manager husband told her a few months ago that he needed to focus all his energy on becoming an “AI native” and requested that she take on almost all parenting responsibilities for the couple’s preschool-age daughter. She complied.

sentences.en: A 31-year-old tech start-up worker living in San Francisco, who spoke on the condition of anonymity for fear of professional repercussions, said that her engineering manager husband told her a few months ago that he needed to focus all his energy on becoming an “AI native” and requested that she take on almost all parenting responsibilities for the couple’s preschool-age daughter.
sentences.zh: 一位居住在旧金山的 31 岁科技初创公司员工因担心职业影响而不愿透露姓名，她表示，她的工程经理丈夫几个月前告诉她，他需要集中所有精力成为“人工智能原住民”，并要求她承担起夫妻俩学龄前女儿的几乎所有养育责任。

sentences.en: She complied.
sentences.zh: 她答应了。

blocks.html_en: She described the experience as surreal, as he spent days, nights and weekends, locked in his office toiling away on AI projects. But her husband eventually thanked her: He was now the top user of AI in his company.

sentences.en: She described the experience as surreal, as he spent days, nights and weekends, locked in his office toiling away on AI projects.
sentences.zh: 她将这段经历形容为超现实的，因为他日日夜夜和周末都被锁在办公室里埋头苦干人工智能项目。

sentences.en: But her husband eventually thanked her: He was now the top user of AI in his company.
sentences.zh: 但她的丈夫最终感谢了她：他现在是公司人工智能的顶级用户。

blocks.html_en: Still, the couple, who earn a combined income of $550,000, say they feel woefully behind their friends, many of whom work at OpenAI and Anthropic and are expecting big payouts when their companies go public. “I have this dream house that I see on Redfin but I know I won’t get to it because Anthropic people will get to it first,” she said.

sentences.en: Still, the couple, who earn a combined income of $550,000, say they feel woefully behind their friends, many of whom work at OpenAI and Anthropic and are expecting big payouts when their companies go public.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，这对总收入为 55 万美元的夫妇表示，他们对自己的朋友感到遗憾，他们中的许多人在 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 工作，并期望在他们的公司上市时获得巨额支出。

sentences.en: “I have this dream house that I see on Redfin but I know I won’t get to it because Anthropic people will get to it first,” she said.
sentences.zh: “我在 Redfin 上看到了这座梦想之家，但我知道我不会到达它，因为人类会首先到达它，”她说。

blocks.html_en: A longtime venture capitalist, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to speak candidly about her financial circumstances, said she and her husband were priced out when looking for a home in San Francisco in the $3 million price range. Many of the homes in her price range bid up to $7 million and $8 million, leading locals here to refer to a “mansion shortage.”

sentences.en: A longtime venture capitalist, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to speak candidly about her financial circumstances, said she and her husband were priced out when looking for a home in San Francisco in the $3 million price range.
sentences.zh: 一位不愿透露姓名的长期风险投资家坦诚地讲述了自己的财务状况，她表示，她和丈夫在旧金山寻找 300 万美元价格范围内的房屋时，被高价淘汰了。

sentences.en: Many of the homes in her price range bid up to $7 million and $8 million, leading locals here to refer to a “mansion shortage.”
sentences.zh: 她的价格范围内的许多房屋竞价高达 700 万美元和 800 万美元，导致当地人称之为“豪宅短缺”。

blocks.html_en: What’s happening in Silicon Valley is a turbocharged version of the division in the American economy between superstars and everyone else.

sentences.en: What’s happening in Silicon Valley is a turbocharged version of the division in the American economy between superstars and everyone else.
sentences.zh: 硅谷正在发生的事情是美国经济中超级巨星和其他人之间分裂的强化版。

blocks.html_en: Superstar companies such as Google, Amazon and Meta won a concentrated share of their markets - and tech stocks have accounted for nearly half of the surge in the S&P 500 since the end of 2010, according to S&P Global Indices. Superstar cities, including in the Bay Area, generate far more economic output per worker and thrive relative to low-productivity ones. About one third of the total personal wealth in the United States is held by the top 1 percent of households, while the bottom half of Americans holds less than 3 percent, according to Federal Reserve data.

sentences.en: Superstar companies such as Google, Amazon and Meta won a concentrated share of their markets - and tech stocks have accounted for nearly half of the surge in the S&P 500 since the end of 2010, according to S&P Global Indices.
sentences.zh: 标普全球指数显示，谷歌、亚马逊和 Meta 等超级明星公司赢得了集中的市场份额，自 2010 年底以来，科技股占标普 500 指数涨幅的近一半。

sentences.en: Superstar cities, including in the Bay Area, generate far more economic output per worker and thrive relative to low-productivity ones.
sentences.zh: 包括湾区在内的超级明星城市，人均经济产出要高得多，而且相对于低生产率城市来说也更繁荣。

sentences.en: About one third of the total personal wealth in the United States is held by the top 1 percent of households, while the bottom half of Americans holds less than 3 percent, according to Federal Reserve data.
sentences.zh: 根据美联储的数据，美国个人财富总额的约三分之一由最富有的 1% 家庭持有，而最底层的一半美国人则持有不到 3%。

blocks.html_en: Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late ’90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he’s hopeful that it’s an early chapter in an evolving story. “It’s happened many times before,” he said, “that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is - into a new wave of prosperity.”

sentences.en: Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late ’90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he’s hopeful that it’s an early chapter in an evolving story.
sentences.zh: 自 90 年代末以来一直在科技界工作的拉斯金表示，尽管当前的时刻让许多人感到不安，但他希望这是一个不断发展的故事的早期章节。

sentences.en: “It’s happened many times before,” he said, “that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is - into a new wave of prosperity.”
sentences.zh: “这种情况以前发生过很多次，”他说，“某些事情发生内爆，所有这些人都失去了工作，但随后人才又进入了下一波新的繁荣浪潮。”

blocks.html_en: While still job hunting, Smith is now volunteering at her church, where she has helped church leaders revamp their use of tech with AI.

sentences.en: While still job hunting, Smith is now volunteering at her church, where she has helped church leaders revamp their use of tech with AI.
sentences.zh: 在寻找工作的同时，史密斯现在在她的教堂做志愿者，帮助教会领袖改进他们对人工智能技术的使用。

blocks.html_en: She’s not sure if she wants to go back to Silicon Valley - or if that’s even possible. “The emperor has no clothes,” she said.

sentences.en: She’s not sure if she wants to go back to Silicon Valley - or if that’s even possible.
sentences.zh: 她不确定自己是否想回到硅谷——或者是否有可能。

sentences.en: “The emperor has no clothes,” she said.
sentences.zh: “皇帝没有穿衣服，”她说。

content_html_zh:
旧金山 - 在近两年的职业生涯中，苏珊·史密斯认为自己是美国经济彩票的赢家。 她在技术工作之间轻松转换，在最近的职位（Meta 的中层高管）中获得了超过 50 万美元的薪水。 作为一名单亲妈妈，史密斯可以负担得起两名保姆的费用并抚养她残疾的儿子，而无需担心经济问题。

但随着硅谷一头扎进人工智能领域并大幅裁员，这种安全感已经消失。 史密斯曾经欣然每周工作 70 小时，她相信这家科技巨头的应用程序造福于人类，而且她的贡献受到高度重视。 这种感觉在她上一个角色中被削弱了，她帮助高管培训新的“人工智能员工”，这是经理们将人工智能系统视为真正的团队成员的内部命令的一部分。

她在 Meta 经历了四轮裁员，但在 5 月份，公司解雇了她。

现在，史密斯面临着一个与那个富裕时代完全不同的就业市场。 她的领域几乎没有职位空缺，而且她申请的几个地方都没有回复她。 她的前夫是 Meta 的一名设计师，于 2020 年被解雇，最终放弃了寻找自己专业的工作。 他现在在仓库搬运箱子。

自近 20 年前的大衰退以来，随着该行业在经济上加速远离美国其他地区，政治和企业领导人将硅谷誉为美国活力的亮点。 美国年轻人，尤其是精英大学的年轻人，纷纷越过华尔街涌向科技领域，使计算机科学成为自 2008 年以来美国大学中增长最快的大型专业。

闯入 Meta 的行列、谷歌或 SpaceX 提供了一种轻松过度的陷阱，并进入了硅谷企业宗教信仰，即随机的软件小部件可以改变世界。

从大多数客观标准来看，美国的科技之都仍然是经济的超级赢家。 只有工人们说他们不这么认为。 他们根深蒂固的乐观情绪已经让位于一种感觉，即饥饿游戏级别的稀缺现在已成为新常态。

史密斯认为自己属于幻灭者之一，尽管她说她对技术本身并不悲观。 “我个人并不认为仅仅为了让几个人成为四倍万亿富翁而这样做有什么意义，”她说。

随着人工智能的传播，美国职业精英的不稳定感可能是美国其他地区未来前景的预演。 科技企业家阿尼尔·达什 (Anil Dash) 表示，硅谷技术人员“是技术人员想要为每个人做的事情的小白鼠”。

在科技公司，痴迷于赢得人工智能竞赛的领导者已责成他们的程序员、律师和人力资源专业人员成为这场变革的前线。 他们的衡量标准是他们能够以多快的速度实现自己的工作自动化，同时看着他们的同事在连续的裁员浪潮中被淘汰。 追踪已宣布裁员的 Layoffs.fyi 统计，自 2022 年以来，已有超过 80 万名科技员工被解雇，其中包括 Meta、微软、甲骨文和亚马逊近几个月的大规模裁员。 （亚马逊执行董事长杰夫·贝佐斯拥有《华盛顿邮报》。）

奥利弗·拉斯金 (Oliver Raskin) 创立了硅谷市场研究咨询公司 Signalcraft Insights，并对科技劳动力的态度进行了调查。

他说，在 ChatGPT 的早期，拉斯金的调查显示出广泛的乐观情绪。 现在他说他相信这种情绪已经改变。

“所有这些人…… 拥有一些中上阶层最令人垂涎​​、最有保障的工作……这些人做了每个人都告诉他们要做的事情：努力学习，得到这些工作，然后你就会得到回报，”拉斯金说。 “突然之间，他们发现自己处于这一转变的最前线。”

这种存在主义恐慌发生在本应令人欣喜的时刻，科技股继续保持 15 年的增长势头，包括 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 在内的一系列人工智能公司正准备以极高的估值上市。 （《华盛顿邮报》与 ChatGPT 的创建者 OpenAI 建立了内容合作伙伴关系。）

但业内资深人士表示，与前几波首次公开募股不同的是，新一轮首次公开募股的财务回报将使一小部分人获得惊人的财富。 这让本已富裕的同龄人对自己的未来感到担忧，加剧了不平等和不安全感。

[人工智能已经在各个层面重塑美国政治]

劳动力市场历史学家表示，硅谷精英的职业不安使他们与美国工人有一些共同之处，美国工人几十年来一直在忍受工作场所安全的侵蚀。

乔治城大学历史学家约瑟夫·麦卡汀 (Joseph McCartin) 表示，20 世纪中后期的汽车工人是那个时代的技术工人。 他说，制造业被认为是国家身份和经济活力的核心，而汽车工作在许多社区被认为是稳定、高薪和高地位的。

麦卡汀表示，从 20 世纪 70 年代开始，随着装配线自动化，汽车工人花了大约两代人的时间才真正失去了优势，他预测人工智能将比这更快地影响白领工作。 “尤其是人工智能的兴起，必将彻底改变工作场所，”麦卡汀说。 “但它发生的方式类似于技术如何改变汽车行业。”

纽约城市大学的劳工社会学家露丝·米尔克曼 (Ruth Milkman) 表示，技术工人正面临着其他行业工人长期以来抱怨的情况：工作不稳定或剥夺了他们的自主权。 “低薪工人已经习惯了，”她说。

与此同时，人工智能已将技术工作转变为残酷的环境，曾经备受赞誉的技能被贬低。 公司现在正在将人工智能的使用纳入绩效评估中； Meta、亚马逊和计算机芯片新贵 Cerebras 等巨头已经使用视觉显示器，根据员工与同事相比使用人工智能的程度对他们进行排名。 由于昂贵的技术耗尽了公司的预算，这种尽可能多地使用人工智能的做法现在正在被取消。

达什说，擅长复杂软件编程的人对他们来之不易的技艺拥有地位和自豪。 但越来越多的程序员——科技行业中收入最高的工人——表示担心自动化编码会让他们的一些专业知识变得无关紧要。

“他们有一种自我意识，[他们的编码技能]在世界上将不再重要，”达什说。

专家表示，科技公司的许多裁员可能是前几年过度招聘的后遗症。 而且它们并没有考虑到信息行业近期招聘人数的不稳定增长，其中包括软件开发人员的就业以及媒体和娱乐行业的就业机会。

不过，深入挖掘后，一些经济学家表示，有迹象表明，硅谷和美国经济中其他依赖技术的部分已经达到了一个转折点，它们在不需要那么多人的情况下实现了增长。

这个概念在最近一次在整个科技行业群聊中引起反响的演讲中得到了概括：在演讲中，初创企业孵化器 Y Combinator 的一位合作伙伴预示着新一代人工智能优先的公司将只在“新情况”、“道德考虑”和“高风险时刻”需要人力。

非营利性劳工研究组织 Burning Glass Institute 首席经济学家加德·莱瓦农 (Gad Levanon) 表示，自 2022 年以来，信息行业的工作时间有所下降，而该行业的经济产出每年增长约 8%，是美国经济总体增长率的三倍多。

他表示，这些数据揭示了包括技术和金融在内的行业发生的巨大变化，即用相同或更少的人完成更多的工作，这种变化正在蔓延到其他专业阶层。

“这就是白领和科技工作的新现实：产出增加，员工人数持平或减少，”莱瓦农说。

Anneke Buffone，心理学博士 去年 12 月离开之前，他被招募到 Meta 负责福利和安全问题的工作，他说程序员并不是唯一感到受到挤压的人。

“有很多不确定性，很多恐惧，很多人感觉，如果他们被解雇了，他们可能就找不到另一份工作了，”她说。

她描述了当她试图解释某种特定的人工智能工具是浪费时间时，遭到了经理的抵制。 系统错误太多，她需要重做。

但她的老板“说，‘不，继续更多地使用它。 一切都会变得更好，”她说。 “管理层有一种感觉，当你说人工智能存在所有这些问题时，他们不想听到。” 她说，人工智能让“一切都变得更快。 它创造了一种你可以完成所有这些事情的错觉。 很多时候你只是在照顾人工智能。”

一位居住在旧金山的 31 岁科技初创公司员工因担心职业影响而不愿透露姓名，她表示，她的工程经理丈夫几个月前告诉她，他需要集中所有精力成为“人工智能原住民”，并要求她承担起夫妻俩学龄前女儿的几乎所有养育责任。 她答应了。

她将这段经历形容为超现实的，因为他日日夜夜和周末都被锁在办公室里埋头苦干人工智能项目。 但她的丈夫最终感谢了她：他现在是公司人工智能的顶级用户。

尽管如此，这对总收入为 55 万美元的夫妇表示，他们对自己的朋友感到遗憾，他们中的许多人在 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 工作，并期望在他们的公司上市时获得巨额支出。 “我在 Redfin 上看到了这座梦想之家，但我知道我不会到达它，因为人类会首先到达它，”她说。

一位不愿透露姓名的长期风险投资家坦诚地讲述了自己的财务状况，她表示，她和丈夫在旧金山寻找 300 万美元价格范围内的房屋时，被高价淘汰了。 她的价格范围内的许多房屋竞价高达 700 万美元和 800 万美元，导致当地人称之为“豪宅短缺”。

硅谷正在发生的事情是美国经济中超级巨星和其他人之间分裂的强化版。

标普全球指数显示，谷歌、亚马逊和 Meta 等超级明星公司赢得了集中的市场份额，自 2010 年底以来，科技股占标普 500 指数涨幅的近一半。 包括湾区在内的超级明星城市，人均经济产出要高得多，而且相对于低生产率城市来说也更繁荣。 根据美联储的数据，美国个人财富总额的约三分之一由最富有的 1% 家庭持有，而最底层的一半美国人则持有不到 3%。

自 90 年代末以来一直在科技界工作的拉斯金表示，尽管当前的时刻让许多人感到不安，但他希望这是一个不断发展的故事的早期章节。 “这种情况以前发生过很多次，”他说，“某些事情发生内爆，所有这些人都失去了工作，但随后人才又进入了下一波新的繁荣浪潮。”

在寻找工作的同时，史密斯现在在她的教堂做志愿者，帮助教会领袖改进他们对人工智能技术的使用。

她不确定自己是否想回到硅谷——或者是否有可能。 “皇帝没有穿衣服，”她说。
